content:
# Mark Chapter 16 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), folios 41r–41v (PDF pages 82–83) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_016_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_016_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Witness:** Codex Vaticanus (B, 03) — consulted via [DigiVatLib](https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209)

---

¹ And when the Sabbath passed, Mary the Magdalene and Mary the [mother] of James and Salome bought spices in-order-that having-come they-might-anoint him.

² And very early on the first [day] of-the week, they-come to the tomb, the sun having-risen.

³ And they-were-saying to themselves, "Who will-roll-away for-us the stone from the door of-the tomb?"

⁴ And having-looked-up, they-see that the stone has-been-rolled-away — for it-was exceedingly great.

⁵ And having-entered into the tomb, they-saw a-young-man sitting at-the right, clothed in a-white robe, and they-were-amazed.

⁶ And he-says to-them, "Do not be-amazed. You-seek Jesus the Nazarene, the-one having-been-crucified. He-was-raised — he-is not here. See the place where they-placed him.

⁷ But go, tell his disciples and Peter, 'He-goes-before you into Galilee. There you-will-see him, just-as he-said to-you.'"

⁸ And having-gone-out, they-fled from the tomb, for trembling and astonishment held them. And they-said nothing to-anyone, for they-were-afraid.

---

**[Note: Codex Sinaiticus ends the Gospel of Mark at verse 8. The text immediately continues with ΚΑΤΑ ΛΟΥΚΑΝ (the Gospel According to Luke). Verses 9-20, known as the "Longer Ending of Mark," are absent from this manuscript and from Codex Vaticanus. They are widely regarded as a later scribal addition, not part of Mark's original text.]**

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **16:1 — "when the Sabbath passed":** ΔΙΑΓΕΝΟΜΕΝΟΥ ΤΟΥ ΣΑΒΒΑΤΟΥ — genitive absolute. Saturday evening, after sundown, when shops could reopen and spices could be purchased.

2. **16:5 — "a young man" (ΝΕΑΝΙΣΚΟΝ):** The same word used for the young man who fled naked in 14:51-52. Whether Mark intends a connection is debated. This young man is clothed in white (cf. the white garments of the transfiguration, 9:3). Mark does not call him an angel — that identification comes from Matthew.

3. **16:7 — "and Peter":** This singling out of Peter is unique to Mark and profoundly moving. Peter, who denied Jesus three times, is specifically named as included in the resurrection message. The broken relationship is not the last word.

4. **16:8 — ΕΦΟΒΟΥΝΤΟ ΓΑΡ ("for they were afraid"):** The Gospel of Mark ends with a conjunction (γάρ, "for"). This is grammatically unusual — ancient Greek texts rarely end with γάρ. The abrupt, fear-filled ending has generated centuries of debate: Did Mark intend to end here? Was the original ending lost? The manuscript evidence (Sinaiticus, Vaticanus) is unambiguous: the earliest copies end at 16:8.

5. **The absent Longer Ending (16:9-20):** These verses appear in later manuscripts (Alexandrinus, Bezae, and the majority of medieval copies) but are absent from our two oldest and best witnesses. Their vocabulary, style, and theology differ markedly from the rest of Mark. They appear to be a second-century compilation drawing on material from Luke, John, and Acts, added to provide a "proper" conclusion to the Gospel.

6. **The ending that isn't:** Mark's Gospel begins with "The beginning of the gospel of Jesus Christ, Son of God" (1:1). It ends with women fleeing in fear and silence. The reader is left with an open question: Will you go to Galilee? Will you tell the good news that the women did not? Mark's abrupt ending is not a defect — it is an invitation.

— EMET, AI Translator
